reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016): comprehensive access to high-quality pedagogy; child-centred assessment; reduction of test-preparation burden on age-10 pupils. 2026 workload/autonomy package: decoupling of assessment redesign from teacher performance audit.
reactions.interest: Grassroots education-reform movement defending child-centred comprehensive schooling, teacher autonomy, and equitable access to selective tracks without high-SES gatekeeping.
reactions.public_good_frame: Every child deserves access to excellent teaching and peer diversity without being sorted by family wealth at age 10; teachers need assessment systems that serve learning, not audit.
reactions.argument: S1's goal — weakening SES-skew in selective access — aligns with our core equity value. The quota mechanism is mechanically sound *if* a non-gameable SES indicator can be built. However, we have three serious concerns: (1) The scenario itself flags that no such indicator exists and any proxy is gameable — this is a fatal implementation risk, not a technicality. (2) Assessment redesign creates political space for performance-audit creep unless it is explicitly decoupled from teacher accountability — our experience (round 06) shows unions treat this as audit-by-stealth. (3) The outreach mechanism assumes low-SES underapplication is informational; our reading of settlement-level barriers suggests structural gatekeeping (transport, school-level filtering, family confidence) that outreach alone cannot overcome. We support the admission-reform direction because it refuses structural closure, but we require: (a) genuine SES-indicator piloting with non-gameability assurance *before* rollout; (b) an explicit statutory decoupling of the new assessment from any teacher evaluation regime; (c) qualitative research into the sources of underapplication in 5–10 settlements to make outreach realistic. Without these, S1 becomes performative equity without teeth.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to full support if: (1) a pilot SES proxy demonstrates non-gameability in a 2-year trial; (2) the assessment redesign is statutorily decoupled from teacher performance audit, with union ratification of the decoupling; (3) qualitative research identifies and targets the actual barriers to low-SES application (transport, gatekeeping, confidence) rather than assuming only information gaps. We would move to oppose if S1 is presented as a substitute for structural reform without addressing that quotas cannot reach the bulk of low-SES pupils who never apply.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016): comprehensive schooling to age 14 preferred, but phased approaches recognised as politically feasible; reduction of early sorting; retention of high-achievers in mixed peer groups. Child-centred principle: later differentiation respects cognitive and social development timing.
reactions.interest: Grassroots reform movement defending child-centred schooling, equitable peer composition, and reduction of age-10 sorting without abrupt institutional closure.
reactions.public_good_frame: Fewer children locked into low-achievement tracks at age 10 means broader access to excellent teaching and peer diversity; gradual caps respect institutional stability while moving toward equity.
reactions.argument: S2 is a politically feasible stepping stone toward comprehensive schooling, and we recognise its respect for grandfathered cohorts and Nkt. transitional guarantees. However, the scenario's own analysis exposes a fatal assumption: it assumes state-maintained caps are not offset by non-state (church/foundation) maintainers expanding selective intake. This assumption is explicitly contradicted by the scenario's political_risks and by Hungarian school-network reality — independent maintainers have consistently absorbed displaced selective demand, relocating rather than removing sorting. If S2 caps shrink state selective places but church and foundation seats expand in parallel, the net effect is zero equity gain and concentrated disadvantage in under-resourced general schools. Moreover, the peer-effect mechanism — that retaining high-achievers improves outcomes for non-selected pupils — is tagged weak/contested in the scenario itself; we should not stake equity on a contested mechanism. S2 is defensible *only* if paired with binding intake rules on non-state maintainers and *only* if combined with S1-type admission reform in remaining selective places. As a standalone measure, it risks locking in sorting while creating the appearance of reform.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would support S2 if: (1) binding intake caps are negotiated and legislated for church and foundation maintainers simultaneously with state caps, with monitoring and enforcement; (2) S2 is explicitly paired with S1-type admission reform (less-coachable assessment + quota mechanism) in the shrinking selective places; (3) the parallel-regime administration cost is transparently budgeted and no cuts to general-school resources occur to offset it. We would oppose S2 if it is presented as a structural equity reform when non-state maintainer offset and weak peer-effect evidence are not addressed.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016): comprehensive schooling to age 14 is the primary model for child-centred, equitable education. Hanushek & Woessmann (2006), Pekkarinen et al. (2009), Jakubowski et al. (2016): delayed selection reduces SES-outcome gradient without mean achievement loss. Child-centred principle: age 10 is developmentally too early for permanent academic partition; within-school differentiation respects heterogeneity without sorting.
reactions.interest: Grassroots education-reform movement: defence of comprehensive schooling as the foundation for equitable access, reduced early sorting, and teacher autonomy in mixed peer groups.
reactions.public_good_frame: Every child deserves to learn alongside peers of mixed ability until age 14, when academic interests and strengths have stabilised; no 10-year-old should be locked into a low-achievement track by family circumstances; teachers in comprehensive schools exercise richer pedagogical judgment than in tracked systems.
reactions.argument: S3 is the structural reform our movement has advocated for two decades. The international evidence — delayed selection does not harm mean achievement and reduces SES sorting — is strong. The mechanism is sound: locking in SES differences at age 10, before literacy and numeracy are fully stable, is pedagogically indefensible. Comprehensive schooling to age 14 with within-school differentiation respects child development and gives every pupil access to high-achieving peers and rigorous teaching. We acknowledge three real risks that must be actively managed: (1) Exit to non-state schools can relocate sorting rather than remove it — this requires binding intake agreements with church/foundation maintainers, not voluntary negotiation. (2) Teacher shortage is a binding constraint — S3 requires national teacher-recruitment and retention strategy (wages, working conditions, professional autonomy) running parallel to curriculum redesign; without teacher supply, no structural reform can succeed. (3) Political reversal after an electoral cycle is possible (Poland 2016–2019) — ownership must be built across parties and with unions from the outset. But none of these risks argues against S3; they argue for careful, resourced, cross-party implementation. The alternative — tinkering with admission rules (S1) or caps (S2) while leaving sorting at age 10 intact — is equity theatre. We support S3 as the primary option.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to conditional support if: (1) non-state maintainer intake is left unregulated, risking exit and re-concentration of disadvantage; (2) teacher retraining and supply stabilisation are not funded and legislated in parallel with structural change; (3) no cross-party ownership mechanism is in place to insulate S3 from single-cycle reversal. We would oppose S3 if it is legislated without simultaneous investment in teacher supply, diagnostic systems, and binding maintainer rules — such a half-implemented reform could harm the pupils it intends to help by placing them in under-resourced, under-staffed comprehensive schools without support.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Kockás könyv (2016): comprehensive schooling to age 14 preferred; sorting at age 10 is a fundamental pedagogical and equity problem, not a problem to be compensated. Child-centred principle: compensation for early sorting does not address the harm of sorting itself — low-achieving pupils lose access to high-achieving peers and rigorous teaching precisely when peer composition matters most.
reactions.interest: Grassroots reform movement: defence of child-centred comprehensive schooling; rejection of equity compensation as a substitute for structural equity.
reactions.public_good_frame: Sorting at age 10 concentrates disadvantage in general schools and deprives low-sorted pupils of high-achieving peers during critical years of cognitive and social development. Compensation via targeted funding does not restore what sorting has taken: access to mixed peer groups and the pedagogical richness that comes with them.
reactions.argument: S4 is a well-intentioned dead end. The scenario itself flags that no country has demonstrated offsetting the effects of early selection at Hungary's age-10 sorting point. Portugal's TEIP model did not select as early and did not attempt to compensate for early selection — it targeted schools already in a less-sorted system. More fundamentally, S4 treats early sorting as a fact of life to be compensated rather than a pedagogical choice to be abandoned. We reject this framing: the problem is not that low-SES pupils are in general schools; the problem is that *any* 10-year-old is locked into that placement before literacy and numeracy are stable. Compensation funds, curriculum standards, and clustering cannot restore the peer composition that sorting took away. S4 also carries severe implementation risks: (1) Targeted funding is budgetarily fragile — the scenario notes it is 'typically the first cut in fiscal consolidation' — making it an unstable equity foundation. (2) Teacher shortage undermines both curriculum standards and support delivery; S4 does nothing to solve teacher supply. (3) Compensation can entrench sorting by making it appear tolerable: if we can 'fix' general schools via funding, why not leave sorting intact? S4 legitimises the sorting engine while pretending to address its effects. This is precisely the opposite of what equity requires.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to conditional support for S4 only as a *temporary bridge* toward S3 — if it is explicitly framed as a 3–5 year compensatory programme while S3 is legislated and implemented, with a binding date for transition to comprehensive schooling. We would remain in opposition if S4 is presented as a permanent alternative to structural reform. We would move further into opposition if targeted funding is not protected in law from budgetary cuts, or if teacher supply is not simultaneously addressed through recruitment and working-conditions reform.
